name: Las Puentes de Malpaso Trail
name_original: Las Puentes de Malpaso – running circular desde Molinaseca por el Camino Francés (PR-LE 42)
description: Jog the difficult 6.9-mile Las Puentes de Malpaso Trail near Molinaseca, featuring medieval bridges and river valley views.
region_intro: Jogging around Molinaseca offers a diverse landscape for runners. The region is characterized by river valleys, including the Boeza and Sil, and rolling hills that provide varied terrain. Runners can find routes that follow the water's edge as well as paths that ascend into the surrounding rural areas. Best jogging routes around Molinaseca The most popular jogging route is Las Puentes de Malpaso Trail, a 6.9 miles (11.2 km) trail that takes 1 hour 29 minutes to complete. This route features varied terrain with notable elevation changes. Another top favourite among local runners is Pajariel Peak – Boeza Bridge loop from Ponferrada, a difficult 9.6 miles (15.5 km) path. This route includes a significant climb to a peak and views of the Boeza river. Local runners also love the Puente del Centenario – Río Sil Trail loop from Ponferrada, a 3.7 miles (6.0 km) trail leading through riverside landscapes, often completed in about 37 minutes. Jogging around Molinaseca is defined by river paths, rural trails, and ascents into the local hills. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside runs to more challenging routes with elevation gain. The routes in Molinaseca are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.1 stars from 6 reviews. More than 600 runners have used komoot to explore Molinaseca's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: Are there many running routes available around Molinaseca?
A: Yes, Molinaseca offers a wide selection of running trails. There are over 70 routes in the area, catering to various fitness levels and preferences.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on Molinaseca's jogging trails?
A: The region is characterized by diverse terrain, including scenic river valleys like the Boeza and Sil, as well as rolling hills. You'll find routes that follow the water's edge and paths that ascend into the surrounding rural areas, offering varied challenges.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly running routes in Molinaseca?
A: Absolutely. Molinaseca has 5 easy running routes perfect for beginners or those looking for a relaxed jog. These often follow riverside paths, providing a gentler experience.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in Molinaseca?
A: Many of the running paths around Molinaseca are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38153977" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente del Centenario – Río Sil Trail loop from Ponferrada&lt;/a&gt; offers a pleasant riverside circuit.

Q: What are some of the longer or more challenging running trails near Molinaseca?
A: For a more demanding run, consider routes with significant elevation changes. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1055984936" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Las Puentes de Malpaso Trail&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 6.9-mile (11.2 km) route with notable climbs. Another challenging option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40011447" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pajariel Peak – Boeza Bridge loop from Ponferrada&lt;/a&gt;, which includes a substantial ascent to a peak.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in Molinaseca?
A: The running routes in Molinaseca are highly regarded by the komoot community, holding an average score of 4.1 stars from 6 reviews. Over 600 runners have explored the varied terrain, often praising the scenic river paths and diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or sights to see along the running routes?
A: Yes, the region offers several points of interest. Depending on your chosen route, you might encounter landmarks like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/716062" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cruz de Ferro (Iron Cross)&lt;/a&gt; or enjoy panoramic views such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5190249" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Montes de León&lt;/a&gt;. Some trails also lead towards &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6188040" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pajariel Peak&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: What is the typical length of running routes in Molinaseca?
A: The lengths of running routes vary significantly. You can find shorter loops, like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38153977" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Puente del Centenario – Río Sil Trail loop from Ponferrada&lt;/a&gt; at 3.7 miles (6.0 km), to longer, more challenging runs such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31881496" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque del Oeste, Ponferrada – Río Sil Trail loop from Ponferrada&lt;/a&gt;, which spans 7.2 miles (11.6 km).

Q: Are there any running routes that follow the Boeza River?
A: Yes, the Boeza River is a prominent feature in the region, and several running routes incorporate its banks. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31471170" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Boeza Bridge loop from Campo&lt;/a&gt; is a great option that follows the river, offering pleasant riverside views.

Q: What are the best times of year for running in Molinaseca?
A: Molinaseca's climate, with its river valleys and rolling hills, generally offers pleasant conditions for running during spring and autumn. Summers can be warm, making early mornings or late evenings ideal, while winters are typically mild but can have cooler temperatures, especially at higher elevations.

Q: Are there any routes suitable for families with children?
A: While specific 'family-friendly' designations aren't always explicit, the easier and shorter riverside routes, often with minimal elevation gain, are generally suitable for families looking for a gentle jog or walk together. Look for routes marked as 'easy' in the komoot app.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the running trails in Molinaseca?
A: Many trails in rural areas like Molinaseca are dog-friendly, especially if your dog is well-behaved and kept on a leash. It's always advisable to check local regulations or specific route details on komoot for any restrictions, particularly in nature reserves or protected areas.
tips: Nice enclave of the Las Puentes de Malpaso route when we cross the Meruelo river.
Popular trail with good views near the town of Riego de Ambrós, where the French Way of Saint James passes.

The Sanctuary of Nuestra Señora de las Angustias in Molinaseca is closely linked to the Camino de Santiago since it is one of the most important points on the route that connects Astorga with Santo Tomás de las Ollas through the Foncebadón pass.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of this trail for jogging?
faqs.answer: This trail is rated as difficult for jogging. While some sources describe it as medium or low difficulty for hikers, the varied terrain, including narrow and rocky sections, ascents, and descents, makes it more challenging for runners. It's best suited for those with some trail running experience.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the route?
faqs.answer: You'll encounter a mix of surfaces, including dirt paths, rocky sections that can be slippery when wet, and areas with roots. The path gradually gains height above the Meruelo River, with some uphill climbs, and includes sections described as 'airy paths'.

faqs.question: What are the main points of interest along the way?
faqs.answer: The trail is known for its two medieval bridges, the Puente Pequeña and Puente Grande, collectively known as Puentes de Malpaso. You'll also pass by the Leafless Tree on the Camino de Santiago near Riego de Ambrós and start near the Sanctuary of Our Lady of Sorrows in Molinaseca.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect with other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Camino Francés - 06 León a Cacabelos and the Camino de Santiago Francés, which are part of the historic Camino de Santiago pilgrimage route.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog this trail?
faqs.answer: The scenery is particularly vibrant in spring and autumn, making these seasons ideal for jogging. These times offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful natural colors.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail starts in Molinaseca, a charming village. You should be able to find parking within the village itself, which is a popular stop for pilgrims on the Camino de Santiago.

faqs.question: Is the trail dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While the trail offers beautiful scenery, its varied terrain with narrow and rocky sections might be challenging for some dogs, especially if they are not used to trail running. If you bring your dog, ensure they are well-behaved and consider keeping them on a leash due to the presence of other trail users and potential wildlife.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee to use the trail?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits or entrance fees required to access the Las Puentes de Malpaso Trail. It is freely accessible to the public.

faqs.question: What kind of natural scenery can I expect?
faqs.answer: You'll jog through diverse landscapes, including riverine forests with poplars, chestnuts, oaks, and alders, as well as areas of abrupt rock formations and dense scrubland. Expect views of the Meruelo River valley and surrounding mountains, with cultivated trees like cherry and walnut near the villages.

faqs.question: Are there any historical facts about the trail?
faqs.answer: The trail follows an ancient path that may have been used by Romans to transport gold from Las Médulas. It also served as an alternative route for pilgrims on the Camino de Santiago. The medieval bridges, Puentes de Malpaso, are believed to have been built to aid pilgrim passage. The area also has historical significance related to the Spanish Civil War.

faqs.question: Is this a loop trail or an out-and-back route?
faqs.answer: The Las Puentes de Malpaso Trail is a circular route, meaning it's a loop trail that brings you back to your starting point in Molinaseca.
